questions.stem: A 30-year-old man presents to the emergency department after twisting his right ankle while playing football. He is unable to weight bear. On examination, there is swelling over the lateral malleolus and tenderness at the distal fibula. No deformity is noted. An X-ray shows an undisplaced transverse fracture at the level of the ankle joint. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
options: Weber B fracture of the lateral malleolus
Grade II ankle sprain (anterior talofibular ligament)
Weber A fracture of the lateral malleolus
Weber C fracture of the lateral malleolus
questions.explanation: A transverse fracture at the level of the ankle joint (tibiotalar joint) is characteristic of a Weber B fracture. Weber A fractures are below the joint, Weber C are above, and medial malleolus fractures would present with medial tenderness.

questions.stem: A 22-year-old woman presents after a fall from a height. She complains of ankle pain and also notes pain in the proximal lateral aspect of her left leg. Examination reveals ankle swelling, ecchymosis over the medial malleolus, but no bony tenderness over the lateral malleolus. An ankle X-ray shows a small avulsion fracture of the medial malleolus and a widened mortise. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
questions.explanation: The combination of a medial malleolus fracture with a widened mortise and proximal fibular pain suggests a Maisonneuve fracture – a spiral fracture of the proximal fibula with disruption of the syndesmosis. Isolated medial malleolus fracture would not cause proximal pain or widening.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old man slips on ice and lands on his right foot. He presents with a deformed ankle and is unable to move it. X-ray reveals fractures of the medial and lateral malleoli with posterior displacement of the talus. There is a separate triangular fragment visible posteriorly. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
options: Isolated lateral malleolus fracture with dislocation
questions.explanation: The presence of a posterior malleolar fragment in addition to medial and lateral malleolar fractures makes this a trimalleolar fracture. A bimalleolar fracture lacks the posterior fragment, and a pilon fracture involves the tibial plafond.

questions.stem: A 10-year-old boy presents after landing awkwardly from a jump. He has right ankle pain and swelling, but can partially weight bear. An X-ray shows a fracture line through the distal tibial metaphysis that extends into the epiphysis. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
options: Salter-Harris III fracture of the distal tibia
Tillaux fracture of the distal tibia
Triplane fracture of the distal tibia
Salter-Harris II fracture of the distal tibia
questions.explanation: A Salter-Harris II fracture involves the metaphysis and extends into the epiphysis without crossing it, which matches the description. Triplane and Tillaux fractures are more complex and typically seen in older children, and a sprain would not show a fracture line.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old marathon runner presents with a 3-week history of gradually worsening lateral right ankle pain, worse with running and relieved by rest. There is no history of acute injury. On examination, there is point tenderness over the distal fibula with mild swelling but no instability. X-ray shows a linear lucency with periosteal reaction in the distal fibula. What is the single most likely diagnosis?
options: Stress fracture of the distal fibula
questions.explanation: The insidious onset, activity-related pain, and X-ray findings of a lucent line with periosteal reaction are classic for a stress fracture. Peroneal tendinopathy would cause tenderness along the tendon sheath, and an os trigonum would cause posterior impingement pain, not lateral bone tenderness.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old male presents to the Emergency Department after twisting his right ankle playing football. He is unable to bear weight for four steps. Examination reveals tenderness over the lateral malleolus. There is no tenderness over the base of the fifth metatarsal or the navicular. Which investigation is most appropriate to confirm the diagnosis?
options: X-ray of the ankle (AP, lateral, mortise)
questions.explanation: According to the Ottawa ankle rules, the presence of malleolar tenderness and inability to bear weight for four steps mandates ankle X-ray to exclude fracture. X-ray of the foot is indicated only if there is midfoot tenderness. MRI, CT, and ultrasound are not first-line investigations for suspected ankle fracture.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old woman presents with persistent ankle pain and swelling two weeks after an inversion injury. Initial X-rays at the time of injury were reported as normal. She has point tenderness over the talar dome. What is the most appropriate next investigation?
questions.explanation: MRI has high sensitivity for detecting occult fractures and bone bruises, which are common in this scenario and often missed on plain X-ray. CT is less sensitive for bone bruising; bone scan is non-specific and exposes to radiation; repeat X-ray is unlikely to show a subtle fracture.

questions.stem: A 22-year-old rugby player sustains an external rotation injury to his ankle. X-ray shows a Weber B lateral malleolus fracture with a normal medial clear space. He has tenderness over the anterior syndesmosis and a positive external rotation stress test. Which investigation best confirms syndesmotic instability?
questions.explanation: Stress radiography can demonstrate dynamic widening of the tibiofibular clear space, confirming syndesmotic instability that may be missed on static imaging. CT and MRI provide static anatomical information but do not assess dynamic instability. Arthroscopy is invasive; weight-bearing X-ray may not be tolerated acutely.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old male presents with ankle pain after a twisting injury. X-ray of the ankle shows no fracture. On examination, there is tenderness over the medial malleolus and over the proximal fibula. What is the most appropriate next investigation?
options: X-ray of the full-length tibia and fibula
questions.explanation: The combination of medial tenderness and proximal fibular tenderness raises suspicion of a Maisonneuve fracture, which involves a proximal fibula fracture and disruption of the syndesmosis. A full-length tibia and fibula X-ray can reveal the occult proximal fibular fracture. Other investigations would not visualize the whole fibula.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old woman with a trimalleolar ankle fracture is being considered for surgery. The mortise view on plain X-ray reveals a small posterior malleolus fragment. Which investigation best characterizes the size and displacement of the posterior malleolus?
questions.explanation: CT provides accurate cross-sectional imaging to measure fragment size and displacement, which are critical for deciding whether fixation is required. MRI is less useful for cortical bone details; stress views and weight-bearing X-rays do not provide precise measurement; arthroscopy is invasive and not for assessment of fragment size.

questions.stem: A 25-year-old man sustains an inversion injury to his right ankle. X-ray shows an undisplaced, stable Weber A lateral malleolus fracture. There is no tenderness over the medial malleolus and no swelling. What is the most appropriate definitive management?
options: Compression bandage and crutches with no immobilisation
Functional treatment with a walking boot and early weight bearing as tolerated
Open reduction internal fixation with a one-third tubular plate
Below-knee plaster cast, non-weight bearing for 6 weeks
questions.explanation: Stable Weber A fractures are treated functionally with a walking boot to allow early mobilisation. Casting is an alternative but functional bracing is preferred for undisplaced fractures. ORIF is reserved for displaced fractures. No immobilisation risks displacement; external fixation is overly invasive.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old woman presents after a fall, with a swollen, deformed ankle. X-ray shows a displaced bimalleolar fracture (medial malleolus and lateral malleolus). There is no open wound. What is the most appropriate definitive management?
options: Open reduction internal fixation of both malleoli
Functional bracing with a walking boot
questions.explanation: Displaced bimalleolar fractures are unstable and require anatomical reduction and internal fixation (ORIF) to restore joint congruity and allow early motion. Casting leads to malunion. External fixation is for open fractures or severe soft tissue injury. Pinning is less stable than plating.

questions.stem: A 55-year-old man presents after a high-energy motorcycle accident. X-ray shows a trimalleolar ankle fracture with a large posterior malleolar fragment involving 30% of the tibial plafond. CT confirms displacement of 3 mm. What is the most appropriate definitive management?
options: Percutaneous screw fixation of the posterior malleolus alone
Open reduction internal fixation of all three malleoli
Open reduction internal fixation of the posterior malleolus only
questions.explanation: Posterior malleolus fractures involving >25% of the articular surface with displacement >2 mm require ORIF to restore joint congruity and prevent post-traumatic arthritis. Fixation of all three components is necessary for stability. Non-operative treatment leads to malunion.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old footballer sustains a severe eversion ankle injury. X-ray shows a Weber C fracture of the lateral malleolus with clear widening of the medial clear space, indicating a syndesmotic injury. There is no open wound. What is the most appropriate definitive management?
options: Open reduction internal fixation of the lateral malleolus alone
Syndesmotic screw fixation after fibular fixation
Functional bracing with a walking boot
questions.explanation: Weber C fractures with syndesmotic diastasis require syndesmotic screw fixation to stabilise the tibiofibular syndesmosis. Fibular fixation alone does not address the syndesmotic injury. Casting leads to chronic instability.

questions.stem: A 35-year-old active woman lands awkwardly during a run. X-ray shows an avulsion fracture of the medial malleolus with 4 mm displacement. There is no other injury. What is the most appropriate definitive management?
options: Compression bandage and immediate weight bearing
Open reduction internal fixation with tension band wiring or screw
questions.explanation: Displaced avulsion fractures of the medial malleolus (>2 mm) in active patients are best treated with ORIF to restore deltoid ligament tension and prevent nonunion. Casting often leads to displacement. K-wires provide insufficient compression; external fixation is excessive.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old man presents to the ED after twisting his left ankle playing football. He has tenderness over the lateral malleolus and mild swelling. X-ray shows an undisplaced Weber B fracture of the lateral malleolus with no medial tenderness or diastasis. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Discharge with advice and analgesia only
Perform stress views to assess stability
Apply a below-knee backslab and advise non-weightbearing
Request a CT scan to assess syndesmotic injury
Arrange urgent orthopaedic review for surgical fixation
questions.explanation: This is a stable ankle fracture (no medial injury, undisplaced). NICE guidelines recommend immobilisation with a below-knee cast or boot. Stress views are not routinely indicated; CT is overkill; urgent referral not needed; discharge without immobilisation risks displacement.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old woman is brought to the ED after a fall from height. Her right ankle is deformed, and there is a 2 cm wound over the medial malleolus with visible bone. She has intact distal pulses and sensation. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Request urgent X-rays of the ankle
Apply a sterile dressing and splint
questions.explanation: For open fractures, the priority is administration of IV antibiotics within 1 hour (per BOAST guidelines). Wound irrigation and dressing, X-rays, and theatre referral are important but secondary to timely antibiotics.

questions.stem: A 60-year-old man with a bimalleolar ankle fracture treated in a backslab becomes increasingly agitated. His pain is out of proportion to the injury, and he reports severe burning pain. On examination, his foot is swollen and tense, with significant pain on passive extension of the toes. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Remove the backslab and split the bandage
Elevate the limb and apply ice
questions.explanation: The clinical findings suggest compartment syndrome. After removing constrictive dressings (which should be done first), if symptoms persist, the next step is to measure compartment pressures to confirm the diagnosis before fasciotomy. Elevation and ice are contraindicated; morphine would mask pain; ultrasound is not diagnostic.

questions.stem: An 82-year-old woman with dementia and a past medical history of osteoporosis presents after a low-energy fall. She is unable to weight-bear. X-ray shows a displaced trimalleolar ankle fracture. She lives alone and has poor mobility. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Discharge with follow-up in fracture clinic
Refer to orthopaedics for consideration of open reduction and internal fixation
Request a CT scan for pre-operative planning
Apply a below-knee cast and arrange for a walking aid
Prescribe a bisphosphonate to reduce future fracture risk
questions.explanation: Displaced trimalleolar fractures are unstable and typically require surgical fixation to restore joint congruity. While CT may be needed later, the immediate next step is to refer to orthopaedics. Conservative management is unsuitable due to displacement and poor functional outcomes.

questions.stem: A 25-year-old man sustains a severe inversion injury. On arrival, his ankle is obviously deformed with a prominent medial malleolus and the foot is pale. Distal pulses are not palpable. Radiographs confirm a posterior dislocation of the ankle with a fracture of the medial malleolus. What is the most appropriate immediate next step?
options: Perform an immediate closed reduction of the dislocation
Apply a backslab in the current position
Request a CT angiogram to assess vascular injury
questions.explanation: The absence of pulses indicates vascular compromise due to dislocation. Immediate closed reduction is required to restore circulation. Further imaging or anticoagulation would delay revascularisation. Angiography may be considered after reduction if pulses remain absent.

questions.stem: A 30-year-old man presents to the Emergency Department after twisting his right ankle playing football. There is a 2cm laceration over the medial malleolus with visible bone at the base. Which single clinical finding marks this as an emergency?
options: Visible bone at the base of the wound
questions.explanation: An open fracture is an orthopaedic emergency. The key finding is a wound communicating with the fracture site, which in this case is evident by visible bone. This requires urgent debridement and antibiotics to prevent osteomyelitis. While swelling and inability to bear weight are common, they are not emergency-defining features alone.

questions.stem: A 45-year-old female presents after a fall from height, landing on her right foot. She has obvious deformity of the ankle and is in severe pain. On examination, the foot is pale and cool, and capillary refill is delayed. Which single clinical finding most strongly indicates a limb-threatening emergency?
options: Inability to move the ankle joint
Complete loss of sensation in the foot
questions.explanation: In a dislocated ankle fracture, the priority is to assess vascular status. Absent dorsalis pedis pulse indicates arterial compromise, which is an emergency requiring immediate reduction to restore blood flow. While sensation loss can be concerning, it may be due to nerve injury; pulselessness is the critical finding for limb salvage.

questions.stem: A 22-year-old man presents with ankle pain after a skiing injury. X-rays show a minimally displaced lateral malleolus fracture. However, he also complains of pain in his proximal lateral leg. Which single physical examination finding should raise suspicion for a more severe injury?
options: Inability to perform a single-leg stance
questions.explanation: Tenderness over the proximal fibula suggests a Maisonneuve fracture, a proximal fibula fracture associated with a syndesmotic injury and medial malleolus fracture or deltoid ligament rupture. This is easily missed and requires full assessment of the proximal tibiofibular joint. The other findings are less specific for this pattern.

questions.stem: A 28-year-old male sustains a closed bimalleolar ankle fracture in a road traffic accident and is placed in a backslab. Over the next 6 hours, he reports worsening pain despite adequate analgesia. On examination, the toes are held in a flexed position and passive extension of the toes causes severe pain. Which single finding is the red flag for a surgical emergency?
options: Decreased capillary refill in the toes
Haemorrhagic blisters over the fracture site
Pain on passive stretch of the toes
Numbness in the first web space
questions.explanation: Pain on passive stretch of the toes is the classic early sign of compartment syndrome, which can complicate ankle fractures. This finding mandates emergency fasciotomy. Decreased capillary refill is a later sign; numbness and weakness indicate nerve ischemia but are less sensitive early. Haemorrhagic blisters suggest blistering but not necessarily compartment syndrome.

questions.stem: A 65-year-old osteoporotic woman presents after stumbling on the kerb. There is marked swelling and deformity of the left ankle. Neurovascular assessment reveals normal pulses but decreased sensation over the dorsum of the foot and great toe. Which abnormal finding most urgently requires further evaluation?
options: Loss of sensation in the first web space
Absence of swelling over the medial malleolus
Presence of ecchymosis in the arch
questions.explanation: Loss of sensation in the first web space indicates deep peroneal nerve dysfunction, which can occur in ankle fractures with significant swelling or displacement. This may signal impending neuropraxia or compartment syndrome and warrants urgent assessment, including checking for tight compartments. The other findings are common and not immediately ominous.
